<!-- ## Step 3: Finding the Desired Policy {.nav-proposed}

**Goal**: Use active PBL to efficiently discover the user's desired policy $\pi^*$.

- We model preferences according to the Bradley-Terry model for reward $\hat{R}(z_w)$
- $z_w$ being the Isometric log-ratio (ILR) transform of $w \in \Delta^{M-1}$.

We estimate $\hat{R}$ using MAP:
\begin{align}
\hat{R} = \operatorname*{arg\,max}_{R \in \mathbb{R}^{|A|}} P(R \mid D)
\end{align}
where $P(R \mid D)$ is approximated as a Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)$ with $\mu = \hat{R}$.

We learn a Gaussian $\hat{R}$ as a 

Since $w \in \Delta^{M-1}$, we transform the action space of $\mathcal{G}$ to be on the simplex via the Isometric log-ratio (ILR) transform.

::: {.callout-tip title="Definition: Thompson Sampling"}
According $\mathcal{G}$ we define a regret minimization acquistion strategy by choosing the best actions over sampled posteriors.

$$
\hat{R} = \mathcal{G}, w = \operatorname*{arg\,max}_w \hat{R}
$$
:::

[@polar]{style="font-size: 0.6em;"} -->

## Step 3: Finding the Desired Policy {.nav-proposed}

**Goal**: Use active PBL to efficiently discover the user's desired policy $\pi^*$.

<!-- Since $w \in \Delta^{M-1}$, we apply the ILR transform $\varphi: \Delta^{M-1} \to \mathbb{R}^{M-2}$ to obtain an unconstrained action space $\mathcal{A}_z = \{\varphi(w) \mid w \in \Delta^{M-1}\}$. This ensures the GP operates on the intrinsic $(M-2)$-dimensional geometry of the simplex. -->

1. The reward $R: \mathbb{R}^{M} \to \mathbb{R}$ is a Gaussian process, inputs lie in $\Delta^{M-1}$
<!-- $$[\Sigma^{pr}]_{ij} = K_{SE}(\varphi(w_i),\, \varphi(w_j)), \quad K_{SE}(z, z') = \sigma^2 \exp\left(-\frac{\|z - z'\|^2}{2\ell^2}\right)$$ -->

2. Given preference data $\mathcal{D}$, approximate the posterior $P(R \mid \mathcal{D})$ as $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)$ centered on the MAP estimate: $\hat{R} = \operatorname*{arg\,max}_{R \in \mathbb{R}^{|A|}} P(R \mid \mathcal{D})$

3. Apply Thompson sampling on the model to actively for new feedback

::: {.callout-tip title="Acquisition: Thompson Sampling"}
At each iteration $i$, we draw $n$ posterior samples and select actions maximizing each sample:
$$R_i^k \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_\mathcal{S},\, \Sigma_\mathcal{S}), \quad w_i^k = \operatorname*{arg\,max}_{w \in \mathcal{S}}\, R_i^k(w) \quad \forall\, k = 1, \ldots, n$$

in our case, we set $n=2$.
:::

<div style="margin-top: -1em;">
[@polar]{style="font-size: 0.6em;"}
</div>
